sections.hint: The Medulla is the 'Bulb' of the brainstem, extending from the pons to the foramen magnum.

topics.content:
• Pyramids: Ventral swellings containing corticospinal fibers. 90% cross at the Pyramidal Decussation (Lower Medulla).
  • Olives: Oval elevations lateral to pyramids containing the Inferior Olivary Nucleus (Cerebellar motor learning).
  • Sulci: CN XII exits the Anterolateral sulcus; CN IX, X, and XI exit the Posteralateral sulcus.

• Nucleus Ambiguus: Motor to muscles of larynx/pharynx (CN IX, X). Damage = Dysphagia/Hoarseness.
  • Solitary Nucleus: Sensory (Taste/Visceral). CN VII, IX, X.
  • Area Postrema: The 'Vomiting Center' located in the floor of the 4th ventricle.
  • Hypoglossal Nucleus: Motor to the tongue.

neuro.q: A 77-year-old woman has a sudden headache and loses consciousness. The next day, after she had regained consciousness, she was aphasic, a bit somnolent, able to answer questions with “yes” or “no,” but was unable to construct even a short sentence. The woman seems to be fully aware of her situation. MRI revealed a lesion in the cerebral cortex. This lesion is most likely located in which of the following?
neuro.l: Lesion of Broca’s area (inferior frontal gyrus: pars opercularis & triangularis) causes expressive aphasia with preserved comprehension.

neuro.q: Dysdiadochokinesia is an impairment of:
neuro.l: Dysdiadochokinesia is a cerebellar sign characterized by inability to perform rapid alternating movements.

neuro.q: The term lentiform nucleus refers to:
neuro.l: The lentiform nucleus = putamen + globus pallidus, forming part of the basal ganglia.

neuro.q: A 59-year-old right-handed male was admitted to the hospital with a chief complaint of occipital headaches of 3 weeks duration. Two weeks prior to admission, the patient had a sudden onset of diplopia on forward gaze and a sensation of dizziness. One day prior to admission, he noted a sudden onset of ptosis of the right eye. The patient has a history of hypertension of two years. His blood pressure is 180/100 mmHg. He is alert, oriented, and cooperative. His pupils are equal and reactive to light. The uvula deviates to the left side and there is deficient elevation on the right side of the palate. The patient also has nystagmus on left lateral gaze and his right corneal reflex is diminished. His facial expressions are full and symmetric; however, he does not respond to pinprick sensations on the left side of his face and left side of his body. Ataxia and intention tremor are present on his right lower extremity. Position, vibration, and touch modalities are intact throughout. What do you think his diagnosis is based on the options below?
neuro.l: Lateral medullary (PICA) lesion causes ipsilateral facial pain loss, contralateral body pain loss, nucleus ambiguus deficits (dysphagia), and cerebellar signs.

neuro.q: Which of the following statements about the globus pallidus is not true?
a: It is located adjacent (lateral) to the internal capsule
It receives excitatory axons from the caudate and putamen
It is the major outflow nucleus of the corpus striatum
It sends inhibitory axons to the thalamus
neuro.l: The globus pallidus receives inhibitory (GABAergic), not excitatory, input from the striatum (caudate + putamen).

neuro.q: A 50-year-old woman was diagnosed with paralysis of her right arm and leg that later became spastic with increased deep reflexes and a positive Babinski sign. There were also movements on the left extremities. The muscles of the lips and cheeks were weak on the right side, but she could tightly close both eyes and wrinkle her forehead symmetrically. An internuclear ophthalmoplegia was observed in which the left eye would not adduct on attempted lateral gaze to the right although both eyes converged on near objects. There was loss of vibratory sense and discriminative touch from the right side of the body. When protruded, the tongue deviated toward the right. Based on these findings, what is the site of lesion?
neuro.l: Combination of corticospinal, medial lemniscus, cranial nerve (facial weakness), and MLF (INO) signs localizes the lesion to the pons.

neuro.q: Which of the following is not correctly paired?
a: Lentiform nucleus – Globus pallidus plus putamen
Corpus striatum – Caudate nucleus plus lentiform nucleus
Amygdaloid body – Claustrum plus amygdaloid nucleus
neuro.l: The amygdaloid body refers only to the amygdala, not the claustrum.

neuro.q: The prefrontal cortex (areas 9, 10, 11 and 12) is essential for:
neuro.l: Prefrontal cortex controls executive functions like planning and foresight.

neuro.q: Lesions of the superior parietal lobule interfere with the patient's ability to combine touch, pressure, and proprioceptive impulses, so that he is unable to appreciate texture, size, and form. This loss of integration of sensory impulses is called:
neuro.l: Parietal lobe damage causes astereognosis.

neuro.q: Adam is a 60-year-old man who is right-handed and was told in the past by his doctor that he has an irregular heartbeat. One morning, he woke to find that his face drooped on the right side and that he could not move his right arm or right leg. When he tried to call an ambulance for help, he had a great deal of difficulty communicating with the operator. His speech was slurred, nonfluent, and missing some pronouns. When listened to, Adam’s heart rate was irregular. He detected an irregular heartbeat. It was very difficult to understand Adam’s speech because it was halting, with a tendency to repeat the same phrases over and over. He had difficulty repeating specific sentences given to him by the neurologist. He was able to follow commands such as “Touch your right ear with your left hand.” His mouth drooped on the right when he attempted to smile, but his forehead remained symmetric. He could not move his right arm at all, but he was able to wiggle his right leg a little bit. Which of the following language problems does Adam have?
neuro.l: Nonfluent speech with preserved comprehension = Broca’s aphasia.

neuro.q: Cerebral dominance refers to:
a: That hemisphere which is more tactual
That hemisphere which controls left or right-handed writers
That hemisphere where speech and language is controlled
That hemisphere which receives the majority of corpus callosum fibers
neuro.l: Dominant hemisphere controls language.

neuro.q: A 64-year-old woman is admitted to the emergency room after she experienced dizziness. Several days later, a neurological examination reveals that the patient is unable to move her right eye medially. Which of the following is the likely locus of the lesion?
neuro.l: MLF lesion → dorsal pons.

neuro.q: A 77-year-old woman has a sudden headache and loses consciousness. The next day, after she had regained consciousness, she was aphasic, a bit somnolent, able to answer questions with “yes” or “no,” but was unable to construct even a short sentence. The woman seems to be fully aware of her situation. MRI revealed a lesion in the cerebral cortex. This lesion is most likely located in which of the following?
neuro.l: Inferior frontal gyrus (Broca’s area).

neuro.q: A 55-year-old man lost consciousness suddenly... Which syndrome could possibly present these signs and symptoms?
neuro.l: Benedict’s syndrome = CN III + red nucleus.

neuro.q: A 69-year-old man... hemorrhagic stroke in the internal capsule. Which location?
neuro.l: Posterior limb carries motor + sensory.

neuro.q: A 59-year-old morbidly obese man... flailing movements. Which nucleus?
neuro.l: Subthalamic lesion → hemiballismus.

neuro.q: The family of a 55-year-old female... tumor causing motor deficits. Which region?
neuro.l: Precentral gyrus = primary motor cortex.

neuro.q: A lesion in the cerebellopontine angle produces?
a: None of the above is correct
neuro.l: CPA → CN VII & VIII.

neuro.q: A 52-year-old male... tumor causing weakness and facial deficit. Which structure?
neuro.l: Internal capsule carries motor fibers.

neuro.q: After his wife had a stroke... neglect of left side. Which area?
neuro.l: Right parietal lesion → hemispatial neglect.
